coredata.dc:title: Taking full advantage of the structure and multi-activities of mineralized microbial surface-displayed enzymes: Portable three-in-one organophosphate pesticides assay device
coredata.dc:creator: Bian, Xuhui
Chen, Daoyuan
Han, Lei
coredata.dc:description: It is of great significance and challenge to design a portable on-site pesticides assay device with three functions of synchronous pretreatment, rapid colorimetric assay, and detoxication. With the development of microbial surface display technology, it is interesting to take full advantage of enzymes, structures, and mineralized layers of surface display cells. Here, we constructed a portable assay device by the simple sedimentation of the phosphate-mineralized organophosphorus hydrolase-fused cells (M-OPH) on syringe filter. M-OPH was prepared by the technique fusion of microbial surface display and biomineralization. Due to the porous structure of M-OPH layer and the insertion of M-OPH into the filtration pores, the M-OPH layer-modified filter membrane can filter out smaller colored impurities in real samples, showing stronger pretreatment performance. Under the specific and high-active catalysis of M-OPH, the device showed short operation time (50 s), convenient operation, high selectivity, acceptable sensitivity and visualization, for the real-time detection of paraoxon, a typical model of organophosphorus pesticides (OPP). Interestingly, M-OPH exhibited not only 4.6-fold enhanced hydrolase activity, but also remarkable peroxidase-mimicking activity and catalytic reduction activity. So, hydrolysate of OPP could be further oxidized or reduced to low-toxic products under the tandem catalysis of OPH and copper phosphate layer. The proposed three-in-one assay device would open up a practical avenue for on-site assay of pesticides in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microbial surface display
Biomineralization
Organophosphate pesticide
Three-in-one assay device
Pretreatment
Detoxification

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and biomineralization of cell-surface-displayed OPH
The steady-state kinetics study of M-OPH
Detection of paraoxon by the multifunctional filter
Performance evaluation of the multifunctional filter
Tandem detoxication of paraoxon by the multifunctional filter device
Synthesis and structure characterization of M-OPH
Biomineralization enhances catalytic activity and stability of M-OPH
Construction of multifunctional filter and rapid paraoxon assay
Assay performance evaluation and real samples assay
Peroxidase-mimicking activity of M-OPH for the oxidation of PNP
Catalytic activity of M-OPH for the reduction of PNP

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21705087 ), A Project of Shandong Province Higher Educational Science and Technology Program (J18KA067), and Research Foundation for Distinguished Scholars of Qingdao Agricultural University (663-1117015).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of fabrication and application of multifunctional device.

Characterizations of M-OPH. (A) Schematic of the forming mechanism of hybrid spindles. (B) TEM image of original NM-OPH. (C – E) TEM images of M-OPH with different magnifications. (E) is the further-magnification of the square area of (D). (F – H) SEM images of M-OPH with different magnifications. (I) XRD patterns of hybrid spindles and bulk Cu3(PO4)2·3H2O and the standard values of Cu3(PO4)2·3H2O (JCPDS 22–0548).

Enzymatic analysis of M-OPH and NM-OPH. (A) Absorbance spectra of reaction solutions containing paraoxon and different catalysts (M-OPH, NM-OPH and copper phosphate) in PBS (pH 7.4). (B) pH-dependent activities of M-OPH and NM-OPH. (C) Temperature stabilities of M-OPH and NM-OPH. (D − E) The steady-state kinetics of M-OPH and NM-OPH. Michaelis-Menten curves of M-OPH (D) and NM-OPH (E). (F) Comparison of V max and K m values of M-OPH and NM-OPH for paraoxon. Error bars represent the standard deviation from three independent measurements. (G) Schematic of the enhanced activity of OPH after mineralization.

Paraoxon detection with multifunctional device. (A) Schematic of the paraoxon assay on M-OPH-loaded membrane. (B) Absorbance spectra of the filtrates from different concentrations of paraoxon (5–500 μM). (C) Working curve for paraoxon detection. Error bars represent the standard deviation from three independent measurements. (D) The structures of various pesticides. (E) Absorbance spectra of filtrates through M-OPH-loaded filter for above pesticides.

Filtration of various juices by different filters and the morphology of M-OPH-functional filter membrane. (A – C) The photos and the absorbance spectra of cabbage juice (A), spinach juice (B), watermelon juice (C) before and after filtration with various functionalized filters. (D) SEM image of M-OPH-functional filter membrane. (E) SEM image of filter membrane with low dosage of M-OPH. (F) The further-magnification of the square area of (E). The arrows indicate M-OPH embedded into pores of filter membrane.

Degradation and detoxication of paraoxon by the multifunctional filter. (A, B) Absorbance spectra of filtrates through M-OPH-loaded or non-loaded filter for OPP solutions with and without H2O2 (A) and NaBH4 (B). (C) HPLC chromatogram of OPP solution containing M-OPH, NaBH4 or H2O2.

head.ce:title: Taking full advantage of the structure and multi-activities of mineralized microbial surface-displayed enzymes: Portable three-in-one organophosphate pesticides assay device

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, 700 Changcheng Road, Qingdao, Shandong 266109, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, 700 Changcheng Road, Qingdao, Shandong 266109, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is of great significance and challenge to design a portable on-site pesticides assay device with three functions of synchronous pretreatment, rapid colorimetric assay, and detoxication. With the development of microbial surface display technology, it is interesting to take full advantage of enzymes, structures, and mineralized layers of surface display cells. Here, we constructed a portable assay device by the simple sedimentation of the phosphate-mineralized organophosphorus hydrolase-fused cells (M-OPH) on syringe filter. M-OPH was prepared by the technique fusion of microbial surface display and biomineralization. Due to the porous structure of M-OPH layer and the insertion of M-OPH into the filtration pores, the M-OPH layer-modified filter membrane can filter out smaller colored impurities in real samples, showing stronger pretreatment performance. Under the specific and high-active catalysis of M-OPH, the device showed short operation time (50 s), convenient operation, high selectivity, acceptable sensitivity and visualization, for the real-time detection of paraoxon, a typical model of organophosphorus pesticides (OPP). Interestingly, M-OPH exhibited not only 4.6-fold enhanced hydrolase activity, but also remarkable peroxidase-mimicking activity and catalytic reduction activity. So, hydrolysate of OPP could be further oxidized or reduced to low-toxic products under the tandem catalysis of OPH and copper phosphate layer. The proposed three-in-one assay device would open up a practical avenue for on-site assay of pesticides in real samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphorus pesticides (OPP) have been widely utilizing in agriculture production [1–3]. Meanwhile, the extensive use of OPP also seriously harms healthiness, environment and food safety [4–7]. Thus, it is especially significant to realize the rapid detection and effective detoxification of OPP. So far, various assay methods have been developed, such as liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [8,9], surface-enhanced Raman spectrometry [10], fluorescence assay [11–13], spectrophotometry [14,15], chemiluminescence [16], and electrochemical sensing [17–19]. However, these methods generally require tedious sample pretreatment, expensive instruments, and time-consuming assay process, restricting their application for on-site quick detection of pesticides. On the other hand, although some colorimetric assay strategies based on cholinesterase inhibition have been widely studied and even used in the actual detection, they generally have poor selectivity and complex enzyme-inhibition operation [20,21]. Moreover, the color of real samples (such as vegetables and fruits) can generally disturb the results of colorimetric assay. Therefore, it is of great interest and significance to develop simple, anti-interference, high-selective and rapid colorimetric assay platform.

It should be noted that the present OPP assay methods lack the degradation function, and samples containing OPP cannot be subsequently detoxified [22], causing trouble for human health and post-treatment of samples. On the other hand, many OPP degradation methods, including bio-degradation [23], photo-degradation [24], and chemical treatment [25], generally require time-consuming operations, complicated steps, non-portable devices and toxic reagents (strong oxidizer and organic reagents). Therefore, it is imperative to explore a portable, time-saving and dual-use device for the on-site OPP detection and degradation.

Microbial surface display technology allows proteins to be displayed on the surface of microbial cell by fusing them with anchoring proteins, without expensive and complicated purification of proteins [26,27]. Although many cell surface-displayed enzymes have been used as substitutes of free or immobilized enzymes, the function of cell structure was generally neglected [27]. In our previous work, we discovered that the activity of bacteria-displayed enzymes was greatly enhanced after the biomineralization of Co3(PO4)2·8H2O on the surface of cells, where the enzymes showed the allosteric effect from inactive form to active form and the active form would be “fixed” [28]. However, the structure and function of the mineralized layer was not utilized, and even the mineralized layer hinders the analytical performance [26]. Therefore, it is meaningful and interesting to study how to make full use of surface display cells and mineralized layers for the development of analytical methods based on surface display cells.

This work is aimed to explore a portable, simple, time-saving, low-cost and recyclable OPP assay device with three-in-one function including high-efficiency pretreatment, high-selective colorimetric rapid assay, and subsequent detoxification (Fig. 1 ). Bacteria-displayed organophosphorus hydrolase (OPH) was selected, because OPH can specifically catalyze the hydrolyzation of OPP, and overcome the shortages of enzyme-inhibition-based OPP assay [29,30]. As the start of work, the mineralized OPH-fused cells (M-OPH) were prepared successfully by the technique fusion of microbial surface display and biomineralization. After biomineralization of Cu3(PO4)2·3H2O, the catalytic activity of OPH was enhanced, due to the allosteric effect. As the additional bonus, the stability of OPH was also enhanced because of the protection of inorganic phosphate. Further, we constructed a portable assay device with three functions by the simple sedimentation of M-OPH on syringe filter. First, some M-OPH partially inserted into filtration pores, and M-OPH layer was compacted on filter membrane, which resulted in the excellent pretreatment function for real samples containing insoluble or colored impurities. Second, under the specific and high-active catalysis of M-OPH, paraoxon, a typical model of OPP, was rapidly hydrolyzed into yellow p-nitriphenol (PNP) and the visualizable, rapid, selective and sensitive OPP assay was realized. Third, copper phosphate on the surface of cells exhibited the peroxidase-mimicking activity and catalytic reduction activity in the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and sodium borohydride (NaBH4), respectively. So, hydrolysate of OPP could be further oxidized or reduced to low-toxic products under the tandem catalysis of OPH and copper phosphate. This work would open a novel avenue for the practical multifunctional pesticides assay devices.

Copper sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4·5H2O), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and 2,2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonicacid) diammonium salt (ABTS) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent, Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Paraoxon was obtained from Dr. Ehrenstorfer GmbH (Augsburg, Germany), dissolved into pure acetone to prepared mother solution, and then stored at 4 °C in dark. All chemicals were of analytical grade and were used without purification. Poly(ether sulfones) syringe filters (diameter 13 mm, pore size 0.22 μm) were purchased from Tianjin Keyilong Lab Equipment Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Disposable sterile syringes (5 mL) were purchased from Zhengzhou Linker Medical Equipment Co., Ltd. (Zhengzhou, China). All aqueous solutions were prepared with Milli-Q water (18.2 MΩ cm).

The morphologies of M-OPH and filter membranes were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi H-7650, Japan) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-4800, Japan), respectively. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on X-ray diffractometer (Bruker D8 Advance, Germany). The ultraviolet-visible absorption spectrum of reaction solution was recorded by the ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-1800, Japan). Component in filtrate was qualitatively analyzed by high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometer (HPLC). Chromatography was conducted by using an HPLC system (Agilent 1290 infinity, USA) with a diode array detector set to 254 nm and Eclipse XDB-C18 column (4.6 × 150 mm, 5.0 μm) at room temperature. The sample injection volume was 20 μL, and the mobile phase was a mixture of methanol and water at a ratio of 50:50 (v/v) at a flow rate of 1.0 mL/min. The retention times of OPP and PNP are 20.1 min and 7.3 min, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and biomineralization of cell-surface-displayed OPH
ce:section.ce:para: The construction and culture of OPH-fused cells were detailedly described in the previous work.[28] And the obtained cells were harvested by centrifugation (6000 rpm, 3 min), washed and re-suspended by phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4). M-OPH was prepared by the biomineralization of OPH-fused cells in PBS containing copper ions. In briefly, 20 μL of CuSO4·5H2O aqueous solution (50 mM) was added into 2 mL of PBS containing OPH-fused cells (optical density at 600 nm, OD600 = 6) at room temperature. After one day, slight blue precipitates were observed and then the resultant M-OPH materials were centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 5 min and washed by water. The amount of M-OPH was quantified by the corresponding OD600 value of original OPH-fused cells (non-mineralized OPH-fused cells, NM-OPH), in order to facilitate the following experiments.

In a typical experiment, the reaction solution containing paraoxon (1 mM), Cu2+ (50 μM), and M-OPH (OD600 = 0.1) in phosphate buffer (PB, 0.1 M, pH 7.4) was incubated at 25 °C for 15 min. Then, M-OPH was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 5 min to terminate the reaction. The maximum absorbance of the hydrolysate PNP at 410 nm (ε410nm = 18400 M−1 cm−1) was monitored by spectrophotometer.

The influence of pH on activities of M-OPH and NM-OPH was researched by the above assay method, except of the varying pH (2.0–12.0). The pH was controlled by using different buffers, including acetate buffer (pH 2.0–5.0), PB (pH 6.0–8.0), Tris–HCl buffer (pH 9.0–10.0) and NaHCO3–NaOH buffer (pH 11.0–12.0). The maximum activity in each group of experiments was defined as 100 % relative activity. To investigate the temperature stability, M-OPH and NM-OPH was incubated in PB solution (0.1 M, pH 7.4) at different temperatures (4–80 °C) for 1 h. After that, the incubated solutions were cooled on ice and then the remaining relative activities were determined by the above typical assay. The maximum activity in each group of experiments was defined as 100 % relative activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The steady-state kinetics study of M-OPH
ce:section.ce:para: For kinetics parameters, the catalysis reaction velocities of M-OPH and NM-OPH was determined according to the above OPH activity assay, except that the concentrations of paraoxon (0.5–80 μM) were varied. The data were fitted to the Michaelis-Menten equation to calculate the kinetic constants: ν = V max × [S] / (K m + [S]), where ν is the initial reaction velocity, V max is the maximal reaction velocity, [S] is the concentration of substrate paraoxon, and K m is the Michaelis constant.[31]

Typically, the suspension of M-OPH (OD600 = 6, 100 μL) in PBS (pH 7.4) was injected into syringe filter. Special care was taken to avoid the presence of air bubbles in the chamber of filter, which would affect the formation of a homogeneous layer of M-OPH. Subsequently, the modified filter was dried at room temperature. Finally, the multifunctional assay device was obtained successfully and stored at 4 °C when not in use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of paraoxon by the multifunctional filter
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 500 μL PBS solution containing Co2+ (50 μM) and various concentration of paraoxon (5–500 μM) was injected into the multifunctional assay device and kept in the filter chamber for 50 s. Subsequently, 1 mL of air was injected into the M-OPH-coated filter to push out the solution. The reaction solution was collected and detected by the naked eye and spectrophotometer at 410 nm. For each sample, three separate measurements were conducted. The calibration curve was plotted by the linear fitting. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated by the formula of LOD = S/N × σ/S, where S/N is signal-to-noise ratio and is usually set to 3, σ is the standard deviation of blank, and S is slope of calibration curve. After detection, the filter membrane was rinsed with water (3 mL), dried and stored at room temperature for the next assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance evaluation of the multifunctional filter
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the storage stability of the multifunctional assay device, the detection of paraoxon was carried out. Firstly, the assay devices loaded with the fresh M-OPH were stored at 4 °C for the 30 days. After each 10 days, the stored multifunctional assay device was used to detect paraoxon. Under the same assay condition, the filtered solution was collected and determined by spectrophotometer at 410 nm. The activity in initial assay for 0 day was defined as 100 % relative activity.

To evaluate the reusability of the multifunctional assay device, the hydrolysis reaction of paraoxon was carried out in fifteen replications by using the same filter device. Under the same reaction times, the filtered solution was collected and determined by spectrophotometer at 410 nm. After each reaction cycle, the multifunctional filter device was washed with pure water (3 mL) to eliminate any residual substrates, and was dried at room temperature or 4 °C for the next cycle use. The activity in first experiments was defined as 100 % relative activity. All experiments were repeated three times.

To assess the potential applicability, the multifunctional filter device was used for detecting OPP in different real samples (spinach juice, watermelon juice and cabbage-leaf juice). The samples containing OPP were injected into the multifunctional assay devices and kept in the filter chambers for 50 s. Subsequently, 1 mL of air was injected into the M-OPH-coated filter to push out the solution. The above solution detected by the naked eye and spectrophotometer at 410 nm. For each sample, three separate measurements were conducted, and the concentrations of paraoxon were calculated for real samples according to the calibration curve. The recovery was calculated by standard-addition method.

To investigate the peroxidase-mimicking activity of M-OPH, TMB and H2O2 was used as the substrate, where TMB could be oxidized by H2O2 to a colored product (TMB*+) in the catalysis of M-OPH. Typically, the reaction solution containing TMB (0.5 mM), H2O2 (1 mM), and M-OPH (OD600 = 0.1) at pH 4.0 was incubated at room temperature for 30 min, and then centrifuged to remove M-OPH to terminate the reaction. The absorbance of TMB*+ at 650 nm was recorded by the spectrophotometer. To further investigate the peroxidase-mimicking activity of M-OPH, another typical chromogenic substrate ABTS was substituted for TMB, which could be oxidized by H2O2 to a colored oxidative product (ABTS*+) in the catalysis of M-OPH. To further research the peroxidase-mimicking activity of M-OPH, PNP (0.1 mM) was as a substrate used for substitution of some typical chromogenic substrate (TMB and ABTS) and the absorbance change of PNP was measured at 410 nm.

To research the reduction catalytic activity of M-OPH, the reduction of PNP was conducted in the presence of M-OPH and NaBH4. Typically, the reaction solution (500 μL) containing PNP (0.2 mM), NaBH4 (33 mM), and M-OPH (OD600 = 0.1) was incubated at 25 °C for 25 min, and then centrifuged to remove M-OPH to terminate the reaction. The absorbance of PNP was recorded at 410 nm by the spectrophotometer. All experiments were repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tandem detoxication of paraoxon by the multifunctional filter device
ce:section.ce:para: For the tandem detoxication of paraoxon based on OPH activity and peroxidase-like activity, H2O2 (1 mM) was added into the solution (500 μL) containing paraoxon (0.15 mM). Subsequently, the mixture solution was filtered through the multifunctional filter by syringe at an even speed for 3 min.

For the tandem detoxication of paraoxon based on OPH activity and reduction catalysis activity, the mixture solution (500 μL) containing NaBH4 (20 mM) and paraoxon (0.2 mM) was pushed out at an even speed for 2 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and structure characterization of M-OPH
ce:section.ce:para: M-OPH hybrid catalysts were prepared by the biomineralization of OPH-fused cells (Fig. 2 A). First, CuSO4·5H2O was added into phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4) containing OPH-fused cells. After one day of biomineralization at room temperature, some light-blue M-OPH precipitates were observed, and then their morphology was characterized by TEM and SEM. TEM images clearly showed that the morphology of OPH-fused Cell changed from rod-like shape (Fig. 2B) to spindle-like morphology with the diameter of 350–600 nm and the length of 1.3 µm (Fig. 2C and 2D), probably because the deposited phosphate crystals collapsed inwardly at the middle of cell under the lowest energy principle and mechanics principle (Fig. 2A). To confirm the hypothesis, longer mineralization time (2 days) was applied and the collapse at the middle of cell was deeper (Fig. S1). In addition, the inorganic crystals layer showed lamellar structure (Fig. 2E). The uniform spindle-like morphology could be clearly observed from SEM images of M-OPH (Fig. 2F and 2G). Furthermore, the high-magnification SEM image showed the layered structure (Fig. 2H), which agreed well with TEM image (Fig. 2E). All diffraction peaks from XRD patterns of as-prepared M-OPH hybrid materials matched well with those of bulk Cu3(PO4)2·3H2O and JCPDS Card (No. 22–0548), affirming the formation of Cu3(PO4)2·3H2O on the surface of cells and high crystallinity (Fig. 2I) [32]. Notably, in PBS without OPH-fused cells, the Cu3(PO4)2·3H2O crystal could form only at the higher concentration of Cu2+ (10 mM), inferring that the surface of OPH-fused cells could provide nucleation sites and promote growth of inorganic crystals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biomineralization enhances catalytic activity and stability of M-OPH
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the catalytic activity of M-OPH, the hydrolysis of paraoxon was used as a model reaction. As is well known, OPH could specifically cut off the phosphorus-oxygen bond of paraoxon to get the hydrolysate PNP, which has maximum absorption peak at 410 nm [28]. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the absorbance at 410 nm reached about 1.7 in the case of M-OPH, which was 4.6 times higher than that of NM-OPH (0.37). In addition, there was no absorbance peak at 410 nm in the case of Cu3(PO4)2·3H2O without OPH-fused cells, demonstrating that Cu3(PO4)2·3H2O itself had no catalytic activity for the hydrolysis of paraoxon. These results confirm that biomineralization could enhance the catalytic activity of M-OPH, which was different with the conventional enzyme immobilization, as the conventional enzyme immobilization generally decreases the catalytic activity of enzyme [33].

Further, the pH-dependent activities were investigated at various pH (2–12). M-OPH remained over 90 % of its original activity at wider pH range (7–10) than the case of NM-OPH (pH 7–8). M-OPH still remained over 65 % of its original activity at pH 6 and 11, while NM-OPH only remained about 40 % of its original activity at pH 6 and 11 (Fig. 3B). Moreover, the stability of M-OPH was investigated at various temperature (4–80 °C). After biomineralization, OPH showed higher temperature stability (Fig. 3C). M-OPH still remained over 95% of its original activity at 4–50 °C and its absolute activity was obviously higher than that of NM-OPH. These results exhibit that biomineralization could enhance the stability of OPH-fused cell.

To profoundly study the influence of biomineralization on the catalytic activity of OPH, the steady-state kinetics of M-OPH and NM-OPH were investigated. The catalytic activities of M-OPH and NM-OPH under the same concentration were detected by varying the concentration of paraoxon (Fig. 3D and 3E), and the apparent kinetics parameters (V max and K m) of M-OPH and NM-OPH were obtained by the fitting based on Michealis–Menten equation (Fig. 3F). The V max value of OPH obviously increased from 0.0715 μΜ s−1 to 0.162 μΜ s−1 after the biomineralization, which was presumably attributed to the allosteric effect from inactive form to active form after the biomineralization (Fig. 3G). On the other hand, the K m value of OPH weakly increased from 6.75 μΜ to 9.33 μΜ, which was presumably attributed to the decreased accessibility of substrates to the active sites of enzymes after the biomineralization [33]. Furthermore, the catalytic efficiency (V max/K m) of M-OPH (1.73 × 10−2 min−1) was 1.63-fold higher than that of NM-OPH (1.06 × 10−2 min−1), demonstrating that biomineralization could significantly enhanced catalytic efficiency of OPH-fused cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of multifunctional filter and rapid paraoxon assay
ce:section.ce:para: The multifunctional device included multifunctional filter and replaceable injection syringe (Fig. 1). As the core component, multifunctional filter was constructed by the simple sedimentation of M-OPH on the commercial syringe filter (pore size: 0.22 μm). The M-OPH suspension was injected into filter. So, M-OPH was spontaneously immobilized onto the filter membrane via filtration and filter membrane served as a benign support for M-OPH. After condition optimization (Fig. S2 and S3), the obtained filter was applied on the rapid OPP assay. For the proof of concept on OPP assay, paraoxon was used as the model of OPPs. The hydrolysis of paraoxon could be catalyzed by M-OPH on the multifunctional filter to generate PNP (Fig. 4 A). The hydrolysate PNP, which was yellow, could be not only observed by naked eyes but also measured by spectrophotometry. With the increase of the concentration of paraoxon, the color of filtrate solution gradually deepened and the characteristic peak of PNP at 410 nm gradually increased (Fig. 4B). Further, the calibration curve was plotted according to the enhanced absorbance at 410 nm as a function of paraoxon concentration (Fig. 4C). The regression equation was y = 0.00562x + 0.47041 (R2 = 0.998) with the linear range of 5–200 μM and acceptable LOD (0.3 μM, S/N = 3). The operation time (50 s) was lower than that of many colorimetric assay methods based on AChE (70–120 min) [34,35], AChE-CHO (45–60 min) [36,37], and OPH (5–20 min) [38,39] (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay performance evaluation and real samples assay
ce:section.ce:para: As a key parameter of assay method, the selectivity was evaluated by conducting some other pesticides (acetamiprid, aldicarb, carbaryl, chlorpyrifos, dibrom, imidacloprid, nitenpyram, and thiamethoxam) onto proposed assay strategy to replace paraoxon (Fig. 4D). There were no obvious absorption peaks at 410 nm for the other pesticides, even other OPPs including chlorpyrifos and dibrom (Fig. 4E), suggesting that the multifunctional filter had excellent selectivity for paraoxon.

In addition, M-OPH-loaded filter showed good long-term storage stability, and the filter still remained 95% of the initial response for paraoxon after 30 days (Fig. S4). The filter also showed good reusability and there is no measurable decrease in the activity for 15 cycles of utilization (Fig. S5).

To assessing the practicability, the multifunctional device was applied on the detection of paraoxon in different real samples including cabbage, spinach and watermelon (Table S2). The Relative standard deviation (RSD) values were calculated to be within 3.4 %, indicating high precision and good reproducibility of proposed assay strategy. By the standard addition method, the recoveries were 96–104 %, demonstrating the acceptable reliability and accuracy of the multifunctional device. Excitingly, insoluble impurities and pigments in real samples were filtered out during the assay operation, and independent pretreatment of real sample was needless.

To further verify the pretreatment effect of the fabricated assay device, different real juices (cabbage juice, spinach juice and watermelon juice) were filtered by various filters (Fig. 5 A – C). As expected, the commercial filter failed to intercept insoluble impurities and pigments. Although the NM-OPH-modified filter could remove insoluble impurities and the most of pigments in real juices, there were still observable interferences for absorption, especially in spinach juice and watermelon juice. In contrast, M-OPH-modified filter could perfectly remove the insoluble impurities and the pigments in real juices, and there was not observable absorption interference in all three juices. To explain the excellent filtering quality, the morphology of M-OPH-loaded filter membrane was observed on SEM (Fig. 5D). M-OPH uniformly distributed on the filter membrane and M-OPH layer formed 3D porous structure. As shown in cross-sectional SEM (Fig. S6), the thickness of M-OPH layer was approximately 35 μm. In addition, the SEM images of filter membrane loaded by low dosages of M-OPH showed many M-OPH embedded into pores of filter membrane in order that the pore size of the filter membrane was shrunk (Fig. 5E and 5F). Hence, 3D hierarchical porous structure formed on the filter membrane enhanced the filtering quality of commercial filter, which can filter out the smaller impurities, pigments and macromolecules in real samples and simplify the operation.

Taken together, the fabricated assay device showed short operation time (50 s) and eliminated independent pretreatment for the following reasons. (1) Microbial surface display technology not only improved the catalytic activity of whole cell catalyst but also showed better stability than free OPH, reducing the reaction time. (2) The biomineralization of copper phosphate on the surface of OPH-fused cell not only showed the good stability, but also ultra-high catalytic efficiency due to the allosteric effect, further shortening the reaction time. (3) Some M-OPH partially inserted into filtration pores, and M-OPH layer on filter membrane was compacted, which resulted in the high-efficiency synchronous pretreatment function for real samples, saving operation time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase-mimicking activity of M-OPH for the oxidation of PNP
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the peroxidase-mimicking activity of M-OPH, the colorimetric assay of M-OPH was conducted, where peroxidase substrates including TMB and ABTS were catalytically oxidized in the presence of H2O2. As shown in Fig. S7, the absorption peaks of oxidized TMB and ABTS were observed at 650 nm and 420 nm, respectively, demonstrating the inherent activity similar with natural peroxidase [40]. Moreover, there were no obvious absorption peaks when substituting NM-OPH for M-OPH, eliminating the possibility that the peroxidase-mimicking activity resulted from original OPH-fused cell. Further, the catalytic oxidation assay was performed by using PNP as a substrate in the presence of H2O2. The absorbance decrement of PNP at 410 nm was observed in the presence of M-OPH and H2O2, and the decrement rate increased with OD600 of M-OPH (Fig. S8), exhibiting the important role of M-OPH for the catalytic oxidation of PNP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic activity of M-OPH for the reduction of PNP
ce:section.ce:para: Interestingly, we also discovered the catalytic activity of M-OPH for the reduction of PNP. When the NaBH4 as reductant was added, the absorbance of PNP decreased due to the reducibility of NaBH4 itself (Fig. S9). In the presence of M-OPH and NaBH4, the absorption peak of PNP almost disappeared. Moreover, the characteristic absorbance of PNP was decreased gradually over time in the presence of M-OPH and NaBH4 (Fig. S10A), exhibiting the catalytic activity of M-OPH for reduction PNP. Significantly, considering the much higher concentration of NaBH4 (33 mM) than that of PNP (0.2 mM) in the reduction process, the pseudo-first-order kinetics were applied to assess the reduction degradation rate of PNP.[41] As shown in Fig. S10B, according to a good linear correlation of ln (At /A0 ) versus reduction time, the rate constant (k), was obtained to be 4.73 × 10−3 s−1. The above results manifested the excellent catalytic activity of M-OPH for the reduction of PNP.

Considering the peroxidase-mimicking activity of inorganic crystal layer and enzymatic activity of OPH, the assay device based on M-OPH was expected to be employed for the follow-up degradation of paraoxon after the detection of paraoxon. Firstly, M-OPH could catalyze the hydrolysis of paraoxon to the hydrolysate PNP. Subsequently, PNP was further degraded in the presence of H2O2 under the peroxidase-mimicking activity of M-OPH (Fig. 1). Actually, the above two-step reactions simultaneously occurred during filtration. To verify the feasibility of above-mentioned strategy, the paraoxon solution with or without H2O2 was filtered through the multifunctional device with syringe at an even speed for 3 min after condition optimization (Fig. S11 and S12A). As shown in Fig. 6 A, the filtrate without H2O2 showed obvious absorption peak of PNP at 410 nm, which was related to the hydrolase activity of M-OPH. There was no absorption peak in the presence of H2O2, illuminating that the intermediate PNP had been oxidized. The above results confirmed the paraoxon detoxication based on peroxidase-mimicking activity was feasible by using the multifunctional device.

Considering the catalytic reduction activity of inorganic crystal layer and enzymatic activity of OPH, the multifunctional assay device based on M-OPH was expected to be employed for another degradation approach of paraoxon, where the hydrolysis of paraoxon and the reduction of PNP simultaneously occurred during filtration (Fig. 1). To verify the feasibility of above-mentioned strategy, the paraoxon solution with or without NaBH4 was filtered at an even speed for 2 min after condition optimization (Fig. S12B). As shown in Fig. 6B, there was no absorption peak of PNP at 410 nm in the presence of NaBH4, illuminating that the intermediate PNP had been reduced. The above results confirmed the paraoxon detoxication based on the catalytic reduction activity was feasible by using the multifunctional device.

To further prove the degradation of paraoxon, HPLC assay was conducted for various filtrates (Fig. 6C). After the paraoxon solution was flow through the multifunctional filter, the HPLC peak of paraoxon disappeared and the peak of PNP appeared, confirming the excellent hydrolysis efficiency for paraoxon. After the paraoxon solution containing H2O2 was flow through the multifunctional filter, the both peaks of paraoxon and PNP disappeared, and many peaks appeared at much shorter retention time, indicating PNP was oxidized to more polar small molecules. Analogously, after the paraoxon solution containing NaBH4 was flow through the multifunctional filter, the both peaks of paraoxon and PNP were also disappeared. These results demonstrate that paraoxon can be adequately hydrolyzed and detoxified after a few minutes of filtration. Therefore, the proposed device can conveniently realize the rapid dual-model detoxication of OPP by tandem catalysis, because of the ultra-high OPH activity, the peroxidase-mimicking activity and the catalytic reduction activity of M-OPH.

In summary, M-OPH, bio-hybrid whole-cell catalyst, was rationally prepared by the technological convergence of biomineralization and microbial surface display. Unlike traditional immobilization of enzyme, the biomineralization of copper phosphate on the surface of OPH-fused cell not only showed the good stability, but also ultra-high catalytic efficiency due to the allosteric effect. We have demonstrated the fabrication and application of three-in-one assay device with three following functions. (1) Synchronous high-efficiency pretreatment: Due to the porous structure of M-OPH layer and the insertion of M-OPH into the filtration pores, the M-OPH layer-modified filter membrane can filter out smaller colored impurities in real samples, showing stronger pretreatment performance. (2) On-site rapid colorimetric assay: Under the specific and high-active catalysis of M-OPH, the device showed short operation time (50 s), convenient operation, high selectivity, acceptable sensitivity and visualization for the real-time detection of paraoxon, a typical model of OPP. (3) Dual-model detoxification based cascade reaction: Interestingly, copper phosphate layer on the surface of cells exhibited the remarkable peroxidase-mimicking activity and catalytic reduction activity, in order that the hydrolysate of OPP could be further oxidized or reduced to low-toxic products under the tandem catalysis of OPH and copper phosphate layer. Moreover, the proposed assay device also exhibited high selectivity, stability and reusability. The proposed strategy took full advantage of the structure and multi-activities of mineralized microbial surface-displayed enzymes This work provided a novel three-in-one assay device, which would open a practical avenue for assay of pesticides in real samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21705087), A Project of Shandong Province Higher Educational Science and Technology Program (J18KA067), and Research Foundation for Distinguished Scholars of Qingdao Agricultural University (663-1117015).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2021.132317.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
